title: Edge Detection: Sobel Filters
concept: Edge detection filters like the Sobel filter measure how sharply brightness changes between neighboring pixels, highlighting the boundaries where objects meet their background.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a sharpening filter emphasize?

options.text: The differences between a pixel and its neighbors
The file format of the image
The average color of the whole photo

exercises.explain: Right — sharpening emphasizes local differences. Today we'll use that same idea to explicitly find edges.

cards.title: Turning a photo into a line drawing
cards.body: Some apps can turn any photo into what looks like a pencil outline sketch — just the edges, none of the shading. That effect comes from a filter built to do exactly one thing: find where brightness suddenly jumps.

cards.title: What is edge detection?
cards.body: Edge detection filters measure how sharply brightness changes between neighboring pixels. A large jump usually means a real boundary — like where a dark object meets a bright background. The Sobel filter is a classic kernel built specifically to measure this kind of change, separately checking for horizontal and vertical jumps.

cards.title: Feeling for a step under a blanket
cards.body: Imagine running your hand across a blanket draped over furniture. Where the surface is flat, you feel nothing change. Where it suddenly steps up or down, you feel it immediately. A Sobel filter runs its "hand" across brightness the same way, and reports strongly wherever it feels a sudden step.

cards.title: Outlining a coffee cup
cards.body: Point a Sobel filter at a photo of a coffee cup on a plain table. Inside the cup, brightness barely changes — the filter stays quiet. But right at the cup's silhouette, brightness jumps sharply from cup to background, and the filter lights up, tracing the outline.

cards.title: It doesn't know it found a "cup"
cards.body: Edge detection sounds smart, but a Sobel filter has zero understanding of objects. It only measures brightness jumps. A strong shadow, a scratch, or a stripe on a shirt can trigger it just as easily as a real object boundary — it's just following the math.

cards.title: Flat region vs. edge region
cards.body: How Sobel reacts in different spots:

left.text:
Neighboring pixels are similar
Filter output stays near zero

right.text:
Neighboring pixels jump sharply
Filter output spikes strongly

exercises.prompt: What does a Sobel filter detect?

options.text: Places where brightness changes sharply between neighboring pixels
The exact object present in the photo, like "car" or "face"
The file size of the image
The average color across the whole photo

exercises.explain: Correct — Sobel measures sharp brightness changes, which often mark object boundaries.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A Sobel filter can tell the difference between a real object edge and a strong shadow.
exercises.explain: False. Sobel only measures brightness change — it can't tell whether that change came from an object, a shadow, or anything else.

exercises.sentence: In a flat, uniform region of an image, a Sobel filter's output stays close to ___.
exercises.explain: Zero is correct — with barely any brightness change between neighbors, the filter has little to report.

exercises.prompt: Which region of a photo would make a Sobel filter respond most strongly?

options.text: The silhouette line where a dark cup meets a bright tabletop
The middle of a plain, evenly lit wall
A perfectly uniform patch of blue sky

exercises.explain: A sharp brightness jump, like a silhouette against its background, is exactly what triggers a strong Sobel response.

exercises.prompt: You run a Sobel filter on a photo of a zebra. Where will the filter respond most strongly?

options.text: Along the boundaries between the zebra's black and white stripes, where brightness jumps sharply
Only along the outer silhouette of the zebra, ignoring the stripes
Evenly across the entire zebra, since it's one object
Nowhere, because Sobel only works on photos of round objects

exercises.explain: Exactly — Sobel fires wherever brightness jumps, stripes included, not just at the outer outline. Next: a smarter, multi-step recipe that cleans this up into crisp, meaningful edges.
